words.definition: the ability to sustain effort for a long time
example_sentences: Marathon running requires enormous endurance.
She trained specifically for endurance events.
Mental endurance is as important as physical in sport.
words.pronunciation_tip: en-DYOOR-unss — three syllables, stress on second.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'ability to keep going' — say 'endurance'.

words.definition: a competition in which many players take part over several rounds
example_sentences: She reached the final of the tennis tournament.
The football tournament attracted teams from 32 countries.
He's been training hard for the upcoming tournament.
words.pronunciation_tip: TOOR-nuh-munt — three syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'competition with many teams' — say 'tournament'.

words.definition: a person who watches an event without taking part
example_sentences: Thousands of spectators watched from the stands.
The spectators cheered loudly throughout the match.
A large spectator crowd creates an electric atmosphere.
words.pronunciation_tip: spek-TAY-ter — three syllables, stress on second.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'people who watch' — say 'spectators'.

words.definition: being moved to a lower division in sport
example_sentences: The team was devastated by relegation.
They fought hard to avoid relegation all season.
Relegation from the top division means huge financial losses.
words.pronunciation_tip: rel-ih-GAY-shun — four syllables, stress on third.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'moved down to a lower league' — say 'relegation'.

words.definition: causing strong disagreement or debate
example_sentences: The referee's decision was highly controversial.
His comments after the game were deeply controversial.
Controversial calls are unavoidable in sport.
words.pronunciation_tip: kon-truh-VUR-shul — four syllables, stress on third.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'people disagree about it' — say 'it was controversial'.

words.definition: a match or competition that decides who can enter the main event
example_sentences: She won the qualifier and earned a place in the finals.
The qualifier was more difficult than expected.
Three teams failed to make it through the qualifier.
words.pronunciation_tip: KWOL-ih-fy-er — four syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'the match before the real competition' — say 'the qualifier'.
words.collocations: ['win a qualifier', 'qualify through a qualifier', 'tournament qualifier']

words.definition: an activity you enjoy in your free time
example_sentences: His favourite pastime is watching old films.
Gardening has become a popular pastime for many people.
Reading is an excellent pastime for long journeys.
words.pronunciation_tip: PAS-tym — two syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'hobby' every time — 'pastime' is slightly more general.

words.definition: physical or mental strength to sustain effort for a long time
example_sentences: Cyclists need incredible stamina to complete the Tour de France.
She built her stamina through months of regular training.
Mental stamina is as important as physical in chess.
words.pronunciation_tip: STAM-ih-nuh — three syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'energy to keep going' always — say 'stamina'.

words.definition: fair and generous behaviour in sport
example_sentences: He lost gracefully — a true example of good sportsmanship.
The team was praised for their outstanding sportsmanship.
Poor sportsmanship damages the reputation of a sport.
words.pronunciation_tip: SPAWRTS-mun-ship — three syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'behaving well in sport' as a noun — say 'sportsmanship'.

words.definition: a raised platform where the top three finishers stand to receive medals
example_sentences: She stepped onto the podium to collect her gold medal.
He trained for years to reach the Olympic podium.
Only three athletes get to stand on the podium.
words.pronunciation_tip: POH-dee-um — three syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'the place for medal winners' — say 'the podium'.
words.collocations: ['reach the podium', 'stand on the podium', 'podium finish']

words.definition: a player who replaces another during a match
example_sentences: The manager brought on a substitute at half-time.
She was substituted after thirty minutes due to injury.
The substitute scored the winning goal.
words.pronunciation_tip: SUB-stih-tyoot — three syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'the reserve player' — say 'the substitute'.

words.definition: ranking system in tournaments giving top players easier early draws
example_sentences: The top seed goes directly into the second round.
Her seeding means she avoids the best players early on.
Seeding ensures the strongest players don't meet too early.
words.pronunciation_tip: SEE-ding — two syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'ranking in a competition' every time — say 'seeding' or 'she is the top seed'.

words.definition: someone who does an activity for enjoyment not professionally
example_sentences: She plays chess at amateur level.
He started as an amateur but turned professional last year.
Amateur sport is just as competitive as professional.
words.pronunciation_tip: AM-uh-ter — three syllables, stress on first. Don't say 'am-uh-TYOOR' — common mistake.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'not professional' always — say 'amateur'.

words.definition: a person or team expected to lose who sometimes wins
example_sentences: They were the underdogs but they won the championship.
Everyone loves an underdog story.
She was the underdog in every match but reached the final.
words.pronunciation_tip: UN-der-dog — stress on first syllable.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'the weaker team' every time — say 'the underdog'.

words.definition: a return to success after a period of failure or absence
example_sentences: After two years out injured, her comeback was remarkable.
He staged the greatest comeback in sporting history.
The team's comeback in the second half was incredible.
words.pronunciation_tip: KUM-bak — stress on first syllable.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'she returned to sport and was successful' — say 'she made a comeback'.

words.definition: a playing area for sports like football or cricket
example_sentences: The pitch was in poor condition after all the rain.
They trained on the pitch every morning.
A football pitch is 100 metres long.
words.pronunciation_tip: PICH — one syllable.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'the field' for football in British English — say 'the pitch'.

words.definition: a game in which both teams finish with equal score
example_sentences: The match ended in a 2-2 draw.
They drew against the top team — an impressive result.
A draw meant both teams progressed to the next round.
words.pronunciation_tip: DRAW — one syllable.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'a tie' every time in British English — say 'a draw'.
words.collocations: ['end in a draw', 'draw a match', 'goalless draw']

words.definition: continued effort despite difficulty
example_sentences: Her perseverance through injury made her story remarkable.
Success in sport requires talent and perseverance.
Perseverance is more important than natural ability.
words.pronunciation_tip: pur-sih-VEER-unss — four syllables, stress on third.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'she kept trying' as a noun — say 'she showed perseverance'.

words.definition: the place where an event or competition takes place
example_sentences: The venue for the final has not yet been announced.
The concert venue holds 20,000 people.
Finding a suitable venue was the first challenge.
words.pronunciation_tip: VEN-yoo — two syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'the place for the event' — say 'the venue'.

words.definition: a plan or method for achieving a particular goal in sport
example_sentences: The manager changed tactics at half-time.
Their tactics were brilliant — they pressed high all game.
Tactics are just as important as physical fitness.
words.pronunciation_tip: TAK-tikss — two syllables, stress on first.
words.common_mistake: Don't say 'plan for the game' — say 'tactics'.
